subject: Cardiology
title: Deep vein thrombosis
preambleMarkdown:
# Deep vein thrombosis

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is an examination-focused draft. Local protocols, medication choices, anticoagulation duration, pregnancy care, cancer-associated thrombosis, thrombophilia testing, thrombolysis, and procedure decisions must be checked against current institutional and national guidance before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is clot formation in the deep venous system, most often in the lower limb. It matters because the clot can embolize to the pulmonary arteries, recur after treatment, or damage venous valves and cause chronic post-thrombotic syndrome. Exam questions usually test three decisions: estimate pretest probability, choose D-dimer versus compression ultrasound correctly, and start the right anticoagulation plan while recognizing limb-threatening or bleeding-risk exceptions.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Think Virchow triad: venous stasis, endothelial injury, and hypercoagulability.
- Use a clinical probability score before ordering D-dimer in stable suspected DVT.
- A negative high-sensitivity D-dimer is useful mainly when pretest probability is low or unlikely.
- Compression ultrasound is the key first-line imaging test for suspected lower-extremity DVT.
- Proximal DVT is more likely than isolated distal calf DVT to embolize and usually requires anticoagulation.
- Massive painful swelling, cyanosis, or threatened limb suggests phlegmasia and needs urgent specialist input.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown: - DVT is thrombosis within a deep vein, usually involving the calf, popliteal, femoral, iliac, pelvic, upper-extremity, or catheter-associated venous system. It is part of venous thromboembolism (VTE), which includes DVT and pulmonary embolism (PE).

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- By location: distal calf DVT, proximal lower-extremity DVT, iliofemoral DVT, upper-extremity DVT, catheter-associated DVT, and unusual-site venous thrombosis.
- By trigger: provoked by a transient risk factor, provoked by a persistent risk factor, cancer-associated, pregnancy-associated, estrogen-associated, or unprovoked.
- By timing: acute, subacute, chronic residual thrombus, recurrent DVT, or post-thrombotic syndrome.
- By severity: uncomplicated DVT, DVT with PE symptoms, extensive iliofemoral DVT, or limb-threatening phlegmasia cerulea dolens.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Risk increases with age, hospitalization, surgery, trauma, active cancer, pregnancy and postpartum state, estrogen therapy, prior VTE, inherited thrombophilia, obesity, inflammatory disease, immobility, long travel, central venous catheters, and acute medical illness. Exam stems often hide the diagnosis in a patient after orthopedic surgery, pelvic surgery, malignancy workup, prolonged bed rest, or recent long-haul travel.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Lower-limb DVT is clinically divided into distal calf veins and proximal veins. Proximal disease includes the popliteal, femoral, common femoral, external iliac, and common iliac veins. Iliofemoral thrombosis can obstruct major venous outflow and produce severe swelling. Upper-extremity DVT often involves the axillary or subclavian veins and is commonly catheter-, pacemaker-, malignancy-, or thoracic-outlet-associated.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Deep veins return blood against gravity using venous valves, calf-muscle pumping, respiratory pressure changes, and low-resistance central venous flow. Stasis reduces washout of activated clotting factors. When anticoagulant and fibrinolytic mechanisms cannot balance thrombin generation, fibrin-rich clot propagates along the venous system.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - DVT forms when Virchow triad is present. Stasis occurs with immobilization, paralysis, long travel, heart failure, or venous obstruction. Endothelial injury occurs after surgery, trauma, central venous catheters, and inflammation. Hypercoagulability occurs with cancer, pregnancy, estrogen, antiphospholipid syndrome, inherited thrombophilia, acute infection, and some chronic inflammatory states. Propagation toward proximal veins increases PE risk.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Common provoking factors include recent surgery, major trauma, hospitalization, reduced mobility, active cancer, pregnancy or postpartum state, estrogen-containing therapy, central venous catheter, prior VTE, acute medical illness, and long-distance travel. Unprovoked DVT means no major transient trigger is found; it raises concern for recurrence risk and, in selected patients, occult cancer or antiphospholipid syndrome.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Strong transient factors: major surgery, major trauma, lower-limb immobilization, hospitalization, and postpartum state.
- Persistent factors: active cancer, antiphospholipid syndrome, chronic inflammatory disease, severe thrombophilia, and chronic immobility.
- Medication and hormone factors: estrogen-containing contraception, hormone therapy, and some cancer therapies.
- Patient factors: older age, obesity, prior VTE, family history, pregnancy, and inherited thrombophilia.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Inherited thrombophilias include factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation, antithrombin deficiency, protein C deficiency, and protein S deficiency. Routine thrombophilia testing is not needed for most provoked DVTs because it rarely changes acute management. Testing is more relevant when results would alter duration of therapy, family counseling, pregnancy planning, or suspicion for antiphospholipid syndrome.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Infection is not the usual cause of DVT, but sepsis, inflammatory endothelial injury, hospitalization, central lines, and septic thrombophlebitis can contribute. Septic thrombophlebitis should be considered when venous thrombosis is associated with bacteremia, infected catheter, persistent fever, or local suppuration.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Unilateral leg swelling, calf or thigh pain, warmth, erythema, tenderness along the deep venous system, or new superficial venous prominence.
- Some DVTs are minimally symptomatic or found during PE evaluation.
- PE symptoms include dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, syncope, tachycardia, hypoxemia, hemoptysis, or shock.
- Iliofemoral DVT can cause whole-leg swelling and severe pain.
- Phlegmasia causes massive swelling, cyanosis, pain, and threatened perfusion.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Ask about onset and location of swelling or pain, prior VTE, recent surgery or trauma, hospitalization, immobility, travel, cancer symptoms or known malignancy, pregnancy/postpartum status, estrogen exposure, central venous catheter, infection, bleeding history, anticoagulant use, kidney or liver disease, and symptoms of PE. Check for contraindications to anticoagulation before treatment decisions.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Compare calf and thigh circumference, inspect for unilateral edema, warmth, erythema, superficial collateral veins, tenderness, cyanosis, and skin compromise. Assess pulse, capillary refill, motor and sensory function if swelling is severe. Check vital signs and oxygen saturation because tachycardia, hypoxemia, hypotension, syncope, or pleuritic pain may indicate PE.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, syncope, hypoxemia, or hypotension suggesting PE.
- Massive painful swelling with cyanosis or neurologic/vascular compromise.
- New DVT while already therapeutic on anticoagulation.
- Active bleeding, severe thrombocytopenia, recent intracranial bleeding, or urgent surgery.
- Pregnancy, active cancer, renal failure, antiphospholipid syndrome, or high-risk drug interactions affecting anticoagulant choice.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Important mimics include ruptured Baker cyst, cellulitis, superficial thrombophlebitis, chronic venous insufficiency, lymphedema, muscle tear or hematoma, compartment syndrome, acute limb ischemia, heart failure, nephrotic syndrome, pelvic mass with venous compression, and post-thrombotic syndrome. Bilateral edema is less typical for isolated DVT and should prompt consideration of systemic causes, but bilateral DVT can occur.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First assess stability and symptoms of PE.
- In stable suspected lower-limb DVT, estimate pretest probability using a structured score such as Wells.
- Low or unlikely probability: use high-sensitivity D-dimer; if negative, DVT can usually be excluded without imaging.
- High or likely probability: proceed to compression ultrasound rather than relying on D-dimer.
- If initial proximal ultrasound is negative but suspicion remains, repeat ultrasound or use whole-leg ultrasound according to local pathway.
- If iliac or pelvic DVT is suspected and leg ultrasound is nondiagnostic, consider CT or MR venography.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Core tests include compression duplex ultrasonography and D-dimer when appropriate by pretest probability. Before anticoagulation, obtain CBC, platelet count, renal function, hepatic function when relevant, baseline coagulation tests if using heparin or warfarin pathways, pregnancy test when relevant, and medication review for interactions. If PE symptoms are present, use PE diagnostic pathways with CT pulmonary angiography, ventilation-perfusion scanning, or bedside echocardiography depending on stability and contraindications.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown: - DVT is diagnosed by objective imaging evidence of noncompressible deep vein segment, intraluminal thrombus, absent or abnormal venous flow, or interval propagation on serial ultrasound. D-dimer is a rule-out test in selected low-probability patients; it does not rule in DVT because it rises with age, inflammation, cancer, pregnancy, surgery, trauma, and hospitalization.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Severity depends on clot location, symptom burden, PE presence, limb threat, bleeding risk, provoking factor, recurrence history, cancer status, pregnancy status, renal function, and whether outpatient treatment is safe. Proximal, iliofemoral, recurrent, cancer-associated, or PE-associated DVT is higher risk than isolated distal calf DVT.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Initial Management

- Assess for PE, limb threat, and bleeding risk. If clinical probability is high and imaging will be delayed, many pathways allow interim anticoagulation unless contraindicated. Use hospital pathways for unstable patients, suspected PE with shock, limb-threatening swelling, severe renal failure, active bleeding risk, pregnancy, or complex cancer-associated thrombosis.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Encourage early ambulation once anticoagulation is started and pain allows. Elevation can reduce swelling. Compression stockings may help selected patients with persistent symptoms, but they are not automatically required for every DVT. Address reversible triggers such as immobility and remove unnecessary central venous catheters when safe.

### Pharmacological Management

- Anticoagulation is the main treatment unless contraindicated. Common options include direct oral anticoagulants, low-molecular-weight heparin, unfractionated heparin, fondaparinux, or warfarin bridging depending on renal function, pregnancy, cancer, antiphospholipid syndrome, drug interactions, cost, adherence, and procedure plans. Typical primary treatment is at least 3 months, with longer or indefinite therapy considered for unprovoked, recurrent, persistent-risk, or selected cancer-associated VTE when bleeding risk is acceptable.

### Procedures

- Inferior vena cava filters are not routine for patients who can receive anticoagulation. They are considered mainly when acute proximal DVT or PE exists and anticoagulation is absolutely contraindicated or has caused major bleeding. Catheter-directed thrombolysis or thrombectomy is reserved for selected patients with severe iliofemoral DVT, low bleeding risk, good functional status, and threatened limb or severe symptoms.

### Surgical Management

- Surgery is uncommon for routine DVT. Urgent vascular or interventional consultation is needed for phlegmasia cerulea dolens, threatened limb, compartment syndrome concern, or extensive iliofemoral thrombosis where invasive clot removal is being considered.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Monitor bleeding, recurrent symptoms, PE symptoms, platelet count when heparin exposure creates HIT risk, renal function for dose selection, hemoglobin if bleeding is possible, medication adherence, and drug interactions. Reassess pain and swelling. Repeat imaging is not routine for every improving patient, but it is used for worsening symptoms, suspected recurrence, or serial surveillance of selected distal DVT.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Follow-up should confirm the provoking factor, planned duration of anticoagulation, bleeding risk, medication access, renal function, and need for cancer or thrombophilia evaluation. At 3 months, reassess whether to stop or continue anticoagulation based on provoked versus unprovoked status, recurrence risk, bleeding risk, and patient preference.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Complications include PE, recurrent DVT, post-thrombotic syndrome, chronic venous insufficiency, venous ulcers, phlegmasia, bleeding from anticoagulation, heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, and anxiety or functional limitation after VTE.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Provoked DVT from a major transient risk factor usually has lower recurrence risk after the trigger resolves than unprovoked or persistent-risk DVT. Cancer-associated, recurrent, proximal, and antiphospholipid-associated VTE have higher recurrence risk. Bleeding risk often determines whether extended anticoagulation is acceptable.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prevention includes VTE risk assessment in hospitalized and postoperative patients, early mobilization, mechanical prophylaxis when anticoagulants are unsafe, pharmacologic prophylaxis when indicated, careful perioperative planning, minimizing unnecessary central lines, weight and activity counseling, and avoiding estrogen therapy in patients with high VTE risk unless a specialist-supported plan exists.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Explain the purpose and duration of anticoagulation, missed-dose instructions, bleeding warning signs, drug and supplement interactions, pregnancy considerations, and when to seek emergency care for PE symptoms. Patients should know that swelling may take weeks to improve and that sudden chest pain, dyspnea, syncope, or coughing blood is urgent.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare limb swelling and pain trends, vital signs, oxygenation, and signs of PE.
- Watch for bleeding, bruising, melena, hematuria, neurologic symptoms, and falling hemoglobin.
- Verify anticoagulant timing around procedures and transitions of care.
- Reinforce adherence and safety instructions before discharge.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Match anticoagulant to renal function, liver function, pregnancy status, cancer status, antiphospholipid syndrome concern, weight extremes, cost, and interactions.
- Check antiplatelets, NSAIDs, azoles, antiepileptics, rifampin-like drugs, HIV therapies, and herbal supplements.
- Confirm whether a loading phase is required for the selected DOAC and whether parenteral lead-in is needed.
- Counsel on bleeding precautions and what to do for missed doses.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Routine dental work usually does not require stopping anticoagulation, but invasive procedures need individualized bleeding-risk planning. Coordinate with the prescribing clinician before interrupting therapy, especially during the first 3 months after acute DVT.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Pediatrics

- Pediatric DVT is often catheter-associated or linked to congenital heart disease, thrombophilia, infection, trauma, surgery, or malignancy. Pediatric hematology input is usually needed for dosing, duration, and follow-up.

### Pregnancy

- Pregnancy and postpartum status increase VTE risk. Low-molecular-weight heparin is commonly used because warfarin and many direct oral anticoagulants are not appropriate in pregnancy. Suspected PE or extensive DVT in pregnancy requires coordinated obstetric and specialist care.

### Older Adults

- Older adults have higher VTE and bleeding risk. Falls, kidney function, cognitive impairment, polypharmacy, frailty, and medication access should shape anticoagulant selection and follow-up.

### Renal Impairment

- Renal impairment affects DOAC and low-molecular-weight heparin dosing and may favor unfractionated heparin in severe disease or when rapid reversibility is needed. Avoid assuming standard outpatient dosing is safe without checking kidney function.

### Hepatic Impairment

- Liver disease can increase both bleeding and thrombosis risk. Coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia, varices, drug metabolism, and albumin changes may affect anticoagulant selection.

### Immunocompromised Patients

- Cancer, transplant, inflammatory disease, central lines, and infection increase thrombosis risk. Drug interactions with chemotherapy, azoles, antivirals, and immunosuppressants are common and should be checked.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Major guidance generally agrees on pretest probability first, D-dimer for selected low-probability patients, compression ultrasound as the main diagnostic test, and anticoagulation as standard treatment for most proximal DVT. Differences appear in serial imaging pathways for isolated distal DVT, extended anticoagulation thresholds, cancer-associated anticoagulant choice, and the role of invasive clot removal.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- D-dimer is for ruling out, not ruling in.
- A negative ultrasound does not always end the workup if pretest probability remains high.
- Proximal DVT and PE symptoms change urgency.
- Do not miss phlegmasia: severe pain, swelling, cyanosis, and threatened perfusion.
- The anticoagulant plan is only complete when duration and follow-up are documented.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ordering D-dimer in a high-probability patient and being falsely reassured.
- Treating cellulitis without considering DVT when unilateral swelling is prominent.
- Forgetting to evaluate PE symptoms in a patient with DVT.
- Starting anticoagulation without checking renal function, pregnancy status, bleeding risk, and interactions.
- Stopping anticoagulation without reassessing whether the event was provoked or unprovoked.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### USMLE Step 1

- Know Virchow triad, factor V Leiden, antiphospholipid syndrome, protein C/S deficiency, antithrombin deficiency, and why venous clots are fibrin-rich.

### USMLE Step 2 CK

- Use Wells score logic, D-dimer only in low-probability settings, and compression ultrasound for likely DVT. Recognize PE symptoms and phlegmasia.

### USMLE Step 3

- Focus on sequencing: interim anticoagulation when imaging is delayed and probability is high, outpatient versus inpatient treatment, duration at 3 months, and follow-up safety.

### ABIM

- Expect nuance around isolated distal DVT, recurrent VTE, antiphospholipid syndrome, cancer-associated thrombosis, renal disease, and extended anticoagulation.

### ABFM

- Prioritize outpatient recognition, pretest probability, ultrasound ordering, anticoagulant counseling, bleeding precautions, and referral triggers.

### Surgery boards

- Know perioperative prophylaxis, postoperative DVT, contraindications to anticoagulation, IVC filter indications, and phlegmasia requiring urgent intervention.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Virchow triad is stasis, endothelial injury, and hypercoagulability.
- Proximal DVT has higher PE risk than isolated distal calf DVT.
- Compression ultrasound is the key diagnostic test.
- D-dimer is sensitive but nonspecific.
- Standard treatment is anticoagulation unless contraindicated.
- At least 3 months of treatment is typical for acute DVT, then reassess recurrence and bleeding risk.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Areas requiring ongoing review include optimal management of isolated distal DVT, best extended-treatment strategy for intermediate-risk patients, anticoagulant choice in complex cancer and renal disease, invasive therapy selection for iliofemoral DVT, and patient-specific prediction of post-thrombotic syndrome.

### Source Excerpt: Deep Venous Thrombosis

_Source lines: 4824-5116._

```text
Deep Venous Thrombosis
A. General Characteristics
1. Cause: Virchow triad (endothelial injury, venous stasis,
hypercoagulability) gives rise to venous thrombosis (see also Table
1-5)
2. Risk factors
a. Age >60 years
b. Malignancy
c. Prior history of deep venous thrombosis (DVT), PE, or varicose
veins

d. Hereditary hypercoagulable states (e.g., factor V Leiden,
proteins C and S deficiency, antithrombin III deficiency)
e. Prolonged immobilization or bed rest
f. Cardiac disease, especially CHF
g. Obesity
h. Major surgery, especially surgery of the pelvis (orthopedic
procedures)
i. Major trauma
j. Pregnancy, oral contraceptives/estrogen use
TABLE 1-5 Diseases of the Venous System
Disease 	Clinical Features 	Diagnosis 	Treatment
Superficial
thrombophlebitis
Local tenderness,
erythema along the
course of a superficial
vein
Clinical
diagnosis
Analgesics, monitor
for spread or cellulitis
Chronic venous
insuficiency
Aching of lower
extremities, worse at
end of day; relieved by
elevation of legs and
worsened by
recumbency; edema,
pigmentation, ulcers
Clinical
diagnosis
Leg elevation, avoid
long periods of
standing, elastic
stockings; if ulcers
develop, Unna boots,
wet-to-dry dressings
Deep venous
thrombosis
Usually asymptomatic;
calf pain may be
present
Duplex, D-
dimer
Anticoagulation
B. Clinical Features
1. Clinical presentation may be subtle
2. Classic findings (all have very low sensitivity and specificity)
a. Lower extremity pain and swelling (worse with dependency/
walking, better with elevation/rest)
b. Homans sign (calf pain on ankle dorsiflexion)
c. Palpable cord
d. Fever
Quick HIT
Only 50% of patients with the classic DVT findings have a DVT, and
only 50% of patients with documented DVT have the classic
findings.

C. Diagnosis
1. Available studies
a. Doppler analysis and Duplex ultrasound
- Initial test for DVT; noninvasive, but highly operator
dependent
- High sensitivity and specificity for detecting proximal
thrombi (popliteal and femoral), not so for distal (calf vein)
thrombi
d. Venography
- Most accurate test for diagnosis of DVT of calf veins
- Invasive and infrequently used
- Allows visualization of the deep and superficial venous
systems, and allows assessment of patency and valvular
competence
h. Impedance plethysmography
- A noninvasive alternative to Doppler ultrasound
- Blood conducts electricity better than soft tissue, so
electrical impedance decreases as blood volume increases
- High sensitivity for proximal DVT, but not for distal DVT
(calf veins)
- Poor specificity because there is a high rate of false positives
- As accurate as Doppler, but less operator dependent
n. D-dimer testing
- Has a very high sensitivity (95%), but low specificity (50%);
can be used to rule out DVT when combined with Doppler
and clinical suspicion
2. Interpretation of diagnostic tests
a. Low or moderate pretest probability: D-dimer can be used to
rule out DVT, or if elevated proceed to ultrasound to rule in or
rule out DVT
b. High pretest probability: proceed to ultrasound. If negative,
consider D-dimer testing, repeating ultrasound in a week, or
additional imaging studies of clot could be more proximal
D. Complications
1. Pulmonary embolism (PE) can originate from the iliofemoral,
pelvic, calf, ovarian, axillary, subclavian, and internal jugular
veins, as well as the inferior vena cava and cavernous sinuses of the
skull-see Chapter 2 for discussion on PE
2. Postthrombotic syndrome (chronic venous insuficiency)
a. Occurs in approximately half of all patients with acute DVT
b. Residual venous obstruction and valvular incompetence lead to
ambulatory HTN (see section Chronic Venous Insuficiency)

3. Phlegmasia cerulea dolens (painful, blue, swollen leg)
a. Occurs in extreme cases of DVT-indicates that major venous
obstruction has occurred. Often associated with malignancy
causing hypercoagulable state.

b. Severe leg edema compromises arterial supply to the limb,
resulting in impaired sensory and motor function.

c. Venous thrombectomy is indicated.

E. Treatment

## 1. Anticoagulation

a. Prevents further propagation of the thrombus
b. Multiple options: DOACs, LMWH, or warfarin
2. Thrombolytic therapy (streptokinase, urokinase, tissue plasminogen
activator [tPA])
a. Speeds up the resolution of clots
b. Indicated mainly for patients with massive PE who are
hemodynamically unstable (hypotension with SBP <90 mm
Hg), and with no contraindications for thrombolytics
c. High risk of intracranial hemorrhage with tPA (1% to 2%)
3. Inferior vena cava filter placement
a. Indicated when there is a contraindication to anticoagulation
(e.g., intracranial hemorrhage). All filters should be removed
when the contraindication to anticoagulation has resolved.

b. Effective only in preventing PE, not DVT
Quick HIT
Low-Molecular-Weight Heparin
- Has longer half-life than unfractionated heparin and can be
dosed once daily or BID
- Can be given on outpatient basis
- No need to follow aPTT levels
- Lower risk of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT)
compared to unfractionated heparin
Chronic Venous Insuficiency (Venous Stasis
Disease)
A. General Characteristics
1. Also referred to as postphlebitic syndrome
2. CVI may involve the superficial, deep, or both venous systems

## 3. Anatomy

a. The lower extremity venous system consists of three systems:
Deep, superficial, and perforating systems. Valves exist in all
three systems, preventing retrograde blood flow.

b. The perforating veins connect the superficial and deep systems.

Valves allow flow from superficial to deep, but not vice versa.

## 4. Pathophysiology

a. History of DVT is the underlying cause in many cases (such a
history might not be documented). This has two major effects:
- It causes destruction of venous valves in the deep venous
system. Valvular incompetence results in gravitational
pressure of the blood column to be transmitted to ankles.

- Valves in the perforator veins are also damaged secondary to
the chronically elevated deep venous pressure, inhibiting
transmission of blood from superficial to deep, as normally
occurs.

d. Leads to ambulatory venous HTN, which has two undesirable
effects:
- Interstitial fluid accumulation, resulting in edema.

- Extravasation of plasma proteins and RBCs into
subcutaneous tissues, resulting in brawny induration and
pigmentation (a brown-black color) of skin.

g. Eventually leads to reduced local capillary blood flow and
hypoxia of tissues.

- Even mild trauma may precipitate tissue death and ulcer
formation.

- Venous ulcers usually develop medially from the instep to
above the ankle, overlying an incompetent perforator vein.

Quick HIT
Many patients with a history of DVT eventually develop CVI (80%).

B. Clinical Features
1. Swelling of the lower leg
a. When chronic, causes an aching or tightness feeling of the
involved leg; often worse at the end of the day
b. Symptoms are worsened by periods of sitting or inactive
standing
c. Leg elevation provides relief of symptoms (the opposite is true
in arterial insuficiency)
2. Chronic changes include:
a. Skin changes

- Skin becomes thin, atrophic, shiny, and cyanotic
- Brawny induration develops with chronicity
d. Venous ulcers
- Less painful than ulcers associated with arterial insuficiency
- Usually located just above the medial malleolus
- Often rapidly recur
Quick HIT
Ulcer formation is directly proportional to the amount of swelling
present.

Quick HIT
When superficial thrombophlebitis occurs in different locations over
a short period of time, think of migratory superficial thrombophlebitis
(secondary to occult malignancy, often of the pancreas-Trousseau
syndrome).

C. Treatment
1. Before the development of ulcers, strict adherence to the following
controls stasis sequelae in most patients.

a. Leg elevation: Periods of leg elevation during the day and
throughout the night to a level above the heart.

b. Avoiding long periods of sitting or standing.

c. Heavy-weight elastic stockings (knee-length) are worn during
waking hours.

2. If ulcers develop, management also entails:
a. Wound care, external compression stockings, etc.

Superficial Thrombophlebitis
A. General Characteristics
1. Virchow triad is again implicated (but pathophysiology is not
entirely clear)
2. In upper extremities, usually occurs at the site of an IV infusion
3. In lower extremities, usually associated with varicose veins (in the
greater saphenous system)-secondary to static blood flow in these
veins
Quick HIT

Two conditions that should not be confused with superficial
thrombophlebitis are cellulitis and lymphangitis. In these conditions,
swelling and erythema are more widespread, and there is no
palpable, indurated vein.

B. Clinical Features
1. Pain, tenderness, induration, and erythema along the course of the
vein
2. A tender cord may be palpated
C. Treatment
1. No anticoagulation is required-rarely causes PE. Only if thrombus
extends into the deep system.

2. Localized thrombophlebitis-a mild analgesic (aspirin or NSAIDs)
elevation, and hot compresses; continue activity.

3. Suppurative thrombophlebitis-septic phlebitis is usually due to
infection of an IV cannula. Redness extends beyond the area of the
vein and purulent drainage may be present. Remove the cannula
and administer systemic antibiotics.

Cardiac Neoplasms
- Primary tumors of the heart are rare (typically less than 0.1% of the
general population).

- Metastases from other primary tumors are more common (75% of
cardiac neoplasms). Sites of these primary tumors include the lung,
breast, skin, kidney, lymphomas, and Kaposi sarcoma in patients with
AIDS.
```

### Source Excerpt: Venous Disease: Acute Venous Thromboembolic Disease

_Source lines: 16835-17045._

```text
VENOUS DISEASE: ACUTE VENOUS THROM BOEM BOLIC DISEASE
I. Acute Lower Extremity Deep Vein Thrombosis: Proximal Vessels
A. Epidemiology
l . Incidence of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) : about 250,000 patients per year,
with 200,000 patients also with pulmonary embolism (PE)
2. Risk factors: age, malignancy, immobilization, surgery and trauma, oral con
traceptives, hormone replacement, pregnancy, neurologic disease (spinal cord
injury) , cardiac disease, obesity, and genetic hypercoagulable state (Table 13- 1 6)
B. Pathogenesis and clinical features
l . Most thromboses affect the iliac, femoral, or popliteal lower limb veins. The
deep venous system includes the common femoral, femoral (or superficial
femoral) , deep femoral, popliteal, and tibial veins .

2. After thrombus formation, an acute and chronic inflammatory response leads
to thrombus ampliHcation, organization, and recanalization, often with vein
wall and valve damage.

B e c a u s e of t h e d istal a n d

s u b c lavian a rte ry, its origin is
i n a c c essible by a ste rnotomy
and can b e easily a p p r o a c h e d
by a l eft h i g h a n t e r i o r o r a n
terolateral thoracotomy.

T h ro m b o a n g i itis o b l iterans
ff B u e r g e r d i s e a s e ) : This
unique d i s e a s e affects the
v e n o a rterial plexus, c a u s i n g
d i g ital/distal i s c h e m i a that
resu lts i n pain and g a n g re n e .

It i s stro n g l y a s s o c i ated
with s m o k i n g of a n a d d ictive
nature. Treatment is
s u p p o rtive and i n c l u d e s
s m o k i n g cessati o n .

DVT prophylaxis s h o u l d
b e c o n s i d e r e d for a l l
h o s p italized patients.

P n e u m atic c o m pression
b o ots a n d e a r l y a m b u lation
should b e considered a s
i n itial t h e r a py for DVT
preventio n .

D u p l e x ultraso u n d is the
best way to d i a g n o s e
DVT. C i r c u l ating 0 - d i m e r
m e a s u r e m e n t c a n r u l e
out DVT but, if p o sitive, is
n o n s p e cifi c .

A patient with r e c u rrent P E ,
d e s p ite antic o a g u lation,
should h ave a vena caval
)	filter p l a c e d . -.

## 3 . Vein wall response is highly dependent on endothelial selectins P- and

E-selectin.

4. Presenting symptoms may include unilateral leg pain and swelling, positive
Homans sign (pain on passive dorsiflexion of foot-a nonspecific physical
finding) , or PE.

C . Diagnosis
l . Fifty percent of patients with acute DVT may be asymptomatic.

2. Duplex ultrasound imaging has become the test of choice, with greater than
95% sensitivity and specificity.

3. Diuerential diagnosis of lower extremity pain and swelling includes muscle
strain or contusion, cellulitis, Baker cyst, iliac vein obstruction due to
retroperitoneal tumor or mass, and systemic causes of swelling and edema
(e.g. , congestive heart failure or venous insufHciency) .

D . Management
l . Treatment for acute DVT is anticoagulation. Initial therapy is often with IV
unXactionated heparin (UFH) or subcutaneous low molecular weight heparin
(LMWH) , such as enoxaparin.

a. IV UFH has a narrow therapeutic window and unpredictable dose response.

Heparin is administered continuously, requiring inpatient treatment. It is
useful for patients with renal failure. The therapeutic response is monitored
by following partial thromboplastin time. Patients who do not respond
should have antifactor Xa levels measured.

b . LMWH is more predictable and has greater bioavailability. Administration
is daily or twice a day. LMWH should be avoided in patients with renal
failure. No routine blood monitoring is required.

2. Vena caval interruption via filter placement should be considered when
anticoagulation is contraindicated, PE recurs on anticoagulation, or a
complication develops Xom use of anticoagulation.

a. Filters may be retrievable.

b . Contraindications to anticoagulation include evidence of ongoing bleeding,
trauma resulting in solid organ injury, intracranial hemorrhage, or spinal
hematoma and complication of anticoagulation, such as bleeding.

c. Anticoagulation should be resumed or initiated, when possible, because a
filter alone is not effective treatment of DVT .

## 3 . Systemic or catheter-directed thrombolysis and surgical extraction or

thrombectomy should be used only in patients with massive iliofemoral DVT
at risk of limb gangrene secondary to venous occlusion.

4. Patients who present with idiopathic or recurrent DVT should be evaluated for
hypercoagulable state .

## 5 . Ambulation with DVT does not increase the risk of PE and does decrease

the incidence and severity of chronic venous disease after DVT . Ambulation
and sequential compression therapy are encouraged in the management of
DVT .

6. Long-term treatment
a. Between 1 5 % and 50% of patients often develop symptomatic extension or
recurrent events .

b. Long-term treatment involves anticoagulation, usually with vitamin K
antagonist (warfarin) .

( l ) Patient with a first episode o f DVT with underlying reversible risk factor
should receive 3 to 6 months of treatment.

(2) Patients with a first episode of idiopathic DVT should receive treatment
for 6 to l2 months and should be considered for indefinite anticoagu
lant therapy.

(3) Patients with DVT and malignancy should receive 3 to 6 months of
LMWH and indefinite anticoagulation therapy.

(4) Patients with a Hrst episode of DVT and antiphospholipid antibodies
or two or more thrombophilic conditions should receive l2 months of
therapy and be considered for indeHnite treatment.

V A S C U L A R S U R G E R Y e 235
(5) Patients with a first episode of DVT and a documented thrombophilic
condition should receive 6 to l2 months of treatment and be considered
for indefinite therapy.

(6) Patients with two or more documented episodes of DVT should receive
indefinite treatment.

II. Upper Extremity Deep Venous Thrombosis
A. Thrombotic obstruction of the subclavian, axillary, or brachial vein
l . Usually associated with central venous catheters or other instrumentation
2. Compression in the thoracic outlet, also known as Paget-Schroetter syndrome
or "effort thrombosis"
B. Signs and symptoms are edema, dilated collateral circulation, and pain.

C. Diagnosis is clinical with venous duplex.

D. Management is controversial.

l . Generally, removal of foreign body is sufficient.

2. Treatment may be similar to lower extremity DVT . However, the level of
evidence for this course is lower than for lower extremity DVT .

3 . No reliable evidence is available on superior vena cava interruption.

4. Thrombolysis with 3 to 6 months of anticoagulation is indicated for effort
thrombosis with possible rib resection.

Ill. Distal Lower Extremity Deep Venous Thrombosis
A. Between 1 0% and 15% of patients will propagate to the proximal venous system
and require treatment.

B. DVT of the calf is usually treated expectantly although anticoagulation can be
considered if the patient is symptomatic.

IV. Pulmonary Embolism
A. DVT and PE are different manifestations of a similar disease process, and their
treatment is therefore similar.

B. The majority of patients with proximal DVT also have symptomatic or
asymptomatic PE and vice versa.

C. Patients treated for PE are four times more likely to die of recurrent venous
thromboembolic disease in the next year when compared with patients treated
for DVT ( 1 .5% vs. 0 .4%) .
```

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI. 2025 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2025-guideline-for-the-management-of-patients-with-acute-coronary-syndromes
- AHA/ACC/HFSA. 2022 Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2022-guideline-for-the-management-of-heart-failure
- ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS. 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation. https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001193
- ACC/AHA. 2020 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Valvular Heart Disease. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2020-acc-aha-guideline-for-the-management-of-patients-with-valvular-heart-disease
- ACC/AHA. 2022 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2022-guideline-for-the-diagnosis-and-management-of-aortic-disease
- ACC/AHA. 2024 Guideline for the Management of Lower Extremity Peripheral Artery Disease. https://professional.heart.org/-/media/PHD-Files-2/Science-News/2/2024/2024-PAD-guideline-slide-set.pdf
- NICE. Venous thromboembolic diseases: diagnosis, management and thrombophilia testing. https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng158
- American Society of Hematology. VTE Guidelines: Diagnosis of Venous Thromboembolism. https://www.hematology.org/education/clinicians/guidelines-and-quality-care/clinical-practice-guidelines/venous-thromboembolism-guidelines/diagnosis
- American Society of Hematology. VTE Guidelines: Treatment of DVT and PE. https://www.hematology.org/education/clinicians/guidelines-and-quality-care/clinical-practice-guidelines/venous-thromboembolism-guidelines/treatment
- CHEST. Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE Disease: Second Update of the CHEST Guideline and Expert Panel Report. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34352278/
